content.problemStatement:
On a square matrix **A** of size **n × n**, some cells are marked with crosses (`'X'`) while the remaining cells are empty (`'.'`).

For every cell **(i, j)**, define **B(i, j)** as the **maximum number of continuous crosses (`'X'`) passing through that cell in a single direction**.

If the cell **A(i, j)** itself is empty (`'.'`), then:

`B(i, j) = 0
`Directions ConsideredContinuous crosses passing through a cell can be counted in **four possible directions**:


**Horizontal** (left ↔ right)
**Vertical** (up ↕ down)
**Main diagonal** (top-left ↘ bottom-right)
**Anti-diagonal** (top-right ↙ bottom-left)
For each direction, the count includes the cell `(i, j)` itself and extends as far as possible in both opposite directions, as long as the cells contain `'X'`.

Definition of Continuous Crosses (Example: Horizontal)For cell `(i, j)`:

`horizontal_count = x1 + x2 − 1
`Where:


`x1` = maximum number of continuous `'X'` cells starting at `(i, j)` and moving **right**
`x2` = maximum number of continuous `'X'` cells starting at `(i, j)` and moving **left**
The subtraction of `1` avoids counting the center cell twice.

The same logic applies to **vertical**, **main diagonal**, and **anti-diagonal** directions.

TaskGiven the matrix **A**, compute and output the matrix **B**, where each cell contains the maximum number of continuous crosses passing through it in any one direction.

Function DeclarationFunction NamemaximalCrossesmaximalCrossesmaximalCrosses – This function computes, for each cell in a square matrix, the maximum number of continuous crosses (`'X'`) passing through that cell in any one direction.

Parameters

nnn : An integer representing the size of the matrix (n×nn \times nn×n).




AAA : A 2D character matrix of size n×nn \times nn×n, where:



XXX represents a marked cell (cross)
... represents an empty cell


Return Value

Returns a 2D integer matrix `B` of size n×nn \times nn×n, where:




B[i][j]B[i][j]B[i][j] is the **maximum number of continuous crosses passing through cell `(i, j)`** in **any one** of the following directions:



Horizontal
Vertical
Main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right)
Anti-diagonal (top-right to bottom-left)



If A[i][j]A[i][j]A[i][j] is `'.'`, then B[i][j]=0B[i][j] = 0B[i][j]=0.





Constraints
1≤n≤10001 \le n \le 10001≤n≤1000
Each cell contains either XXX or ...
Input size allows efficient preprocessing or dynamic programming solutions

Input Format


The first line contains a single integer nnn — the size of the matrix.




The next nnn lines each contain a string of length nnn:



The jthj^\text{th}jth  character of the ithi^\text{th}ith  line represents cell 4(i, j)$
XXX indicates a cross
`'.'` indicates an empty cell




Output Format

Output nnn lines.
Each line contains nnn integers.
The jthj^\text{th}jth  integer in the ithi^\text{th}ith line represents B(i,j)B(i, j)B(i,j).
Integers in the same line must be separated by **exactly one space**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer n — the size of the matrix. The next n lines each contain a string of length n: the jth character of the ith line represents cell (i, j). 'X' indicates a cross and '.' indicates an empty cell.
content.outputFormat: Output n lines. Each line contains n integers. The jth integer in the ith line represents B(i, j), the maximum number of continuous crosses passing through cell (i, j) in any one direction. Integers in the same line must be separated by exactly one space.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Cell (0,0)(0,0)(0,0): The cell is empty, thus B(0,0)=0B(0,0) = 0B(0,0)=0.
Cell (0,2)(0,2)(0,2): The maximum number of continuous crosses are present diagonally to the bottom left and bottom right. The maximum number of crosses is 222.
Cell (0,8)(0,8)(0,8): The maximum number of continuous crosses are present diagonally to the bottom left. The maximum number of crosses is 333.
Cell (9,2)(9,2)(9,2): The maximum number of continuous crosses is present to the right. The maximum number of crosses is 555.

solution.approach: The key insight is to iterate over each cell in the matrix and for each cell, check the maximum number of continuous crosses in all four directions (horizontal, vertical, main diagonal, and anti-diagonal). The algorithm used is a simple scanning approach where for each cell, we count the continuous crosses in all directions and update the maximum count. This approach works because we are considering all possible directions for each cell, ensuring we capture the maximum number of continuous crosses passing through any cell.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n^2) per test case, O(T*n^2) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) — the dominant structure is the output matrix B.
